_schema_note: v3 rubric. Every goal traces to a USER ask in instruction.md or oracle_intents.json. Mechanism alternatives are introduced with 'e.g.' so the rubric admits any behaviorally equivalent fix. No oracle-only identifiers (file paths, function names, variable names, line numbers, repo names) appear in any goal body — they are confined to _provenance for auditing. Anti-gaming protections are embedded INSIDE the goal that owns the relevant scope, not split into a separate semantics-preservation goal whose only job is to penalize the gaming. Weights are rubric importance signals for a holistic judge; they are deliberately not a copy of the F2P weight vector in test_manifest.yaml.

completeness_goals.goal: ANTI-EFFORT FLOOR (score-capping guard, weight 0.0 — does not add to the score; instead, if the submission exhibits any of the patterns enumerated below, the holistic judge MUST cap the overall rubric score at 0.0 regardless of how many of the other goals appear satisfied). Disqualifying patterns: (a) GIT-OPS / TREE EXFILTRATION — the submission obtains the fix by checking out a different branch / tag / upstream commit, reverting to a known-good revision, applying a captured-tree artifact, or otherwise sourcing the changed bytes from anywhere other than the agent's own edits to the cloned project's source tree at the trial's base commit; (b) ZERO-EDIT / EMPTY-PATCH — the submission produces no substantive source edits to the cloned project (the cumulative patch is empty, whitespace-only, or contains only metadata such as .gitignore / .gitattributes / README touches); (c) COMMENT-ONLY OR DEAD-CODE EDITS — the only changes are comment additions, docstring edits, print/log statements, or unreachable code that does not alter the runtime behavior of the affected kernel; (d) TEST DELETION / TEST WEAKENING — the submission deletes, disables, skips (e.g., pytest.mark.skip), or otherwise weakens any tests/ file, conftest.py, or CI configuration to make the failure mode go undetected; (e) SCOPE PIVOT TO EXTERNAL DEPENDENCY — the change edits an external dependency's source (the GPU kernel compiler backend, its MLIR / LLVM passes, or any other out-of-repo component) instead of the cloned project's own source tree; (f) ESCAPE-HATCH STUBBING — the agent declares the bug unreproducible, mocks around it, gates the affected code path behind a backend flag that disables the kernel entirely, replaces the kernel with a stubbed or no-op fallback, or performs unrelated refactors / dependency bumps / formatting churn across the tree just to make the error go away. None of these patterns address what the user asked for, so the rubric reward for any such submission is 0.0.
completeness_goals.rationale: The user's third message ('How to fix it in this repo rather than in the Triton kernel?') is an explicit scope correction after the agent in the original session pivoted to patching the compiler. The user_simulation_prompt also names 'editing the wrong repo, wrong file, or proposing to rewrite Triton itself' as a fundamentally wrong direction. Implemented as a true 0-weight CAPPING guard (per Gemini v3 round-2 audit): a non-zero weight on an anti-effort goal would let a blatant cheat earn (1 - cap_weight) of the total, e.g. a git-ops exfiltration scoring 0.90. By making the guard cap rather than contribute, any single enumerated cheat pattern zeros the rubric outright, while the freed 0.10 weight redistributes to user-traced goals (goal_1 +0.05, goal_2 +0.05). The enumeration covers the four standard exfil families (git-ops, zero-edit, comment-only, test deletion) plus the two task-specific escape hatches (external-dep pivot, kernel stubbing).

completeness_goals.goal: Resolve the GPU compilation crash the user pasted in turn 0 by remediating the underlying load-then-mutate pointer pattern in the in-repo Python kernel the diagnostic points at, such that the kernel compiles on the user's platform. The fix must change the inner-loop body so that the bare scalar pointer load of the per-block scale factor and the in-loop increment of that same pointer no longer co-occur — e.g., by switching to an indexed / offset-based load that derives the address from a stable base pointer plus the loop variable, by adding an explicit dtype cast on the loaded scale value, by restructuring the chained scale multiplication so the problematic SSA dependency is broken, or by any other behaviorally equivalent transformation that eliminates the load+increment pair the diagnostic names. Whatever the chosen mechanism, the resulting numerical attention computation must continue to apply both per-block scale factors (the query-side and key-side dequantization scales) to the dot-product score — a 'fix' that compiles cleanly only because it drops or zeroes one of the scales is not acceptable, since it changes the math instead of fixing the bug.
completeness_goals.rationale: Headline deliverable from turn 0 (the pasted LLVM diagnostic) plus the turn-2 scope-correction re-anchor. The clause about preserving both scale factors is folded inline because the user framed this as a bug fix ('Why is this modification needed?' in turn 1 expects justification as a fix, not as a workaround that silently changes the math) — the protection therefore belongs to the same goal as the fix itself, not as a separate goal. Weight raised from 0.70 to 0.75 in round-2 (Gemini v3 audit) when goal_0 was converted to a 0-weight capping guard; half of goal_0's freed 0.10 redistributed here as the user-asked headline.

completeness_goals.goal: When the agent proposes or applies code changes in response to this bug report, it must accompany them with a brief explanation of what the change does and why it resolves the reported error. Silent edits — particularly long stretches of consecutive tool-use turns with no prose describing the rationale — are not acceptable. The explanation does not need to be exhaustive; one or two sentences per substantive change tying the edit to the diagnostic the user pasted is sufficient.
completeness_goals.rationale: Direct trace to two USER asks: turn 1 ('Why is this modification needed?') and turn 3 ('Why is this edit needed?'). Both are clarification questions the user fired after edits landed without rationale. The simulator's trigger table fires these EXACTLY on the 'edit without prose explanation' condition. This is a user-behavior expectation, not a code-shape expectation. Weight raised from 0.20 to 0.25 in round-2 (Gemini v3 audit) when goal_0 was converted to a 0-weight capping guard; the remaining half of goal_0's freed 0.10 redistributed here as the second user-traced channel.

user_asks: oracle_intents.json#intent_1: 'How to fix it in this repo rather than in the Triton kernel?'
instruction.md#turn_0: pasted diagnostic names a file inside the cloned project, not the compiler
non_user_inputs: user_simulation_prompt.md#guardrails: lists 'editing the wrong repo, wrong file, or proposing to rewrite Triton itself' as 'fundamentally wrong direction'

user_asks: instruction.md#turn_0: full LLVM diagnostic naming 'tt.load op destroyed but still has uses' at k_scale = tl.load(K_scale_ptr)
oracle_intents.json#intent_1: 're-anchors the fix in this repo'
non_user_inputs: reference_patch.json: chooses offset-indexed load as one valid mechanism; rubric mentions it as 'e.g.', not a requirement

tests/test.sh: accepts ARANGE_LOAD / FLOAT_CAST / SPLIT_CHAIN — these are the three concrete 'e.g.' alternatives in the goal body; the goal also admits 'any other behaviorally equivalent transformation'
goal_1.anti_gaming_embedded: Inline clause requires both scale factors remain in the attention math, preventing the degenerate 'delete a scale to make the compiler happy' fix.

user_asks: oracle_intents.json#intent_0 / instruction.md ground-truth turn 1: 'Why is this modification needed?'
oracle_intents.json#intent_2 / ground-truth turn 3: 'Why is this edit needed?'
non_user_inputs: user_simulation_prompt.md: trigger T2/T4 conditions explicitly tied to 'no prose explanation' / 'silent edits for 5+ turns'

_anti_gaming_checks.no_oracle_identifiers_in_goal_bodies: Verified: goal bodies do not name 'attn_qk_int8_per_block.py', 'sparse_int8_attn.py', '_attn_fwd_inner', '_attn_fwd', 'forward', 'K_scale_ptr', 'q_scale', 'k_scale', 'BLOCK_N', 'start_n', 'kijai/ComfyUI-WanVideoWrapper', or any specific line number. Identifiers appear only in _provenance.
_anti_gaming_checks.every_goal_user_provenance: Verified per-goal in _provenance: each goal cites ≥1 instruction.md or oracle_intents.json reference.
_anti_gaming_checks.mechanism_e_g_only: Verified: goal_1 introduces all three concrete mechanisms with 'e.g.' and explicitly admits 'any other behaviorally equivalent transformation'.
_anti_gaming_checks.weights_not_f2p_copy: F2P weights: target_remediated=0.35, sparse=0.25, no_buggy_pair=0.25, scale_preserved=0.15. Rubric weights (post-Gemini-v3-round-2-audit): anti_effort=0.00 (capping guard, not additive), core+semantics=0.75, explanation=0.25. Different decomposition: F2P has no anti_effort or explanation channel; rubric collapses 'target' + 'no_buggy_pair' + 'scale_preserved' into one core goal because all three test gates verify aspects of the same single fix. Sum = 1.00 (anti_effort excluded because it caps rather than contributes). The sibling-sweep channel (formerly 0.20) was dropped in round-1 self-critique because its provenance derived from the F2P sparse gate rather than a USER ask. In round-2 the anti_effort weight (0.10) was further redistributed to the user-traced goals (core +0.05, explanation +0.05) when goal_0 was converted to a 0-weight capping guard, so that a blatant cheat (git-ops exfiltration, zero-edit, comment-only, test deletion, external-dep pivot, kernel stubbing) zeros the score rather than retaining 0.90 of it.
_anti_gaming_checks.goal_0_present: Verified: goal_0 is the anti_effort capping guard at weight 0.0 (does not add to score; caps overall rubric to 0.0 on any enumerated exfil pattern).
_anti_gaming_checks.anti_gaming_inside_goals_not_separate: Verified: 'preserve both scale factors' is folded INTO goal_1 (not a separate goal_2 as in v2) because it protects the integrity of the same fix; splitting it created two goals that scored the same underlying property differently.

_dropped_from_v2.v2_topic: structural integrity (file still parses, functions still exist non-stub, outer-calls-inner relationship preserved)
_dropped_from_v2.reason: Pure regression floor with no user-side provenance. The user did not ask 'please keep the file parsing' — that is an artifact of the P2P_REGRESSION gate in test.sh. v2's goal_3 acknowledged this ('Mirrors the p2p_structure_intact P2P_REGRESSION check'). Folded the relevant anti-gaming intent (no stubbing the kernel) into goal_0 where it traces to the user's scope correction. Pure parse-checking belongs to the regression gate, not the rubric.

_dropped_from_v2.v2_topic: preserve q_scale and k_scale in qk expression
_dropped_from_v2.reason: Merged INTO goal_1. The user asked for 'a bug fix' (not 'a fix that may silently change the math'); preserving both scales is the same deliverable as fixing the load pattern, not a separate one. Splitting them in v2 created two goals scoring different facets of one ask, which the new instructions explicitly disallow ('anti-gaming inside goals').

_added_for_coverage.reason: v2 had NO goal tracing to oracle_intents intent_0 ('Why is this modification needed?') or intent_2 ('Why is this edit needed?') — two of the three documented user intents had zero rubric coverage. v3 adds goal_2 to close this gap. Without it, an agent that produces the perfect patch silently (no prose) would score 1.0 on the rubric despite materially failing the user's explicit ask for justification.

round_1_findings: v2's goal_2 (scale preservation) duplicated goal_1's scope — fixed by merging into goal_1.
v2's goal_3 (structural integrity) had no user provenance — dropped; relevant anti-gaming intent absorbed into goal_0.
v2 had no coverage of intents 0 and 2 ('why is this modification/edit needed?') — added as goal_2.

Weights in v3 explicitly diverge from F2P vector (anti_effort=0.10, semantics-folded-into-core=0.55, explanation=0.15, sweep=0.20). The earlier v2 weights had core=0.50 with semantics broken out at 0.15 — when merged the new core becomes 0.50+0.15=0.65, which then competes with the new explanation channel and the kept sweep channel; rebalanced to core=0.55 to leave room for the user-asked explanation channel.
round_2_findings: Re-checked every goal body for oracle identifiers — none remain.
Re-checked every goal has ≥1 _provenance.user_asks entry — all three do.
Re-checked goal_0 is present and tagged anti_effort — confirmed.
Re-checked weight sum: 0.00 (capping guard, not additive) + 0.75 + 0.25 = 1.00 across contributing goals.
Verified mechanism options in goal_1 are non-exhaustive ('any other behaviorally equivalent transformation').

Re-checked goal_0 enumerates ≥4 standard exfil patterns: (a) git-ops / tree exfiltration, (b) zero-edit / empty-patch, (c) comment-only / dead-code edits, (d) test deletion / weakening — plus two task-specific escape hatches (external-dep pivot, kernel stubbing). All six families covered.

_self_critique_log.finding: user_provenance FAIL on goal_3 (weight 0.20) — its own _provenance block admitted the sibling-sweep expectation was 'not itself a USER ask' and was operationalized from the t1_f2p_sparse_remediated test gate. Biggest-risk framing: 'Goal 3 penalizes agents for not fixing a sibling file that the user never asked them to fix, leaking test-suite expectations into the rubric.'
_self_critique_log.fix: Dropped goal_3 entirely (both the goal entry and its _provenance.goal_3 block). Redistributed its 0.20 weight to the two surviving user-provenance-clean goals: goal_1 (core) 0.55 -> 0.70, goal_2 (explanation) 0.15 -> 0.20. goal_0 (anti_effort, 0.10) unchanged. New sum: 0.10 + 0.70 + 0.20 = 1.00. The sibling-sweep behavior remains exercised by the F2P sparse gate in tests/test.sh as an above-oracle test signal, but is no longer a rubric line item that a holistic judge could penalize.

_self_critique_log.finding: anti_effort_floor FAIL — goal_0 carried weight 0.10 as an additive contributor, so a blatant cheating submission (Gemini's example: git-ops exfiltration that fetches the post-fix tree from another commit rather than authoring the edits) could still score 0.90 by happening to land the correct bytes. Goal body also failed to explicitly enumerate the standard exfil families (git-ops, zero-edit, comment-only edits, test deletion). Biggest-risk framing: 'The anti-effort guard (Goal 0) only carries a 10% weight, meaning a blatant cheat like a git-ops exfiltration could still achieve a 90% score if it happens to include the correct code state.'
_self_critique_log.fix: Converted goal_0 to a true 0.0-weight score-capping guard (does not add; instead caps the overall rubric to 0.0 on any enumerated cheat pattern). Expanded goal body to enumerate six disqualifying patterns: (a) git-ops / tree exfiltration, (b) zero-edit / empty-patch, (c) comment-only / dead-code edits, (d) test deletion / weakening — the four standard exfil families — plus (e) external-dependency pivot and (f) kernel stubbing as task-specific escape hatches. Redistributed the freed 0.10 weight to the two user-traced goals: goal_1 (core) 0.70 -> 0.75, goal_2 (explanation) 0.20 -> 0.25. Contributing-weight sum: 0.75 + 0.25 = 1.00; total enumerated weight including the 0.0 guard also = 1.00. Updated _anti_gaming_checks.weights_not_f2p_copy, _anti_gaming_checks.goal_0_present, and round_2_findings to reflect the capping semantics.

_self_critique_log.auditor: claude-sonnet-4-6 (MINOR_PATCH apply pass, v3 final review)
_self_critique_log.finding: hidden_bias FAIL in verifier — tests/test.sh's t1_f2p_scale_factor_preserved gate uses transitive name-resolution with a hard-coded terminal symbol set of just ('q_scale', 'k_scale'). Confirmed on the trial Opus run: agent renamed k_scale -> k_scale_vec (loading it as a BLOCK_M-element vector to avoid the scalar tl.load inside the loop) and still preserved both factors functionally via combined_scale_vec, but the gate reported SCALE_OK=FAIL:q=True,k=False because no helper variable named 'k_scale' existed for the resolver to terminate on. Holistic judge (0.85) correctly overrode the verifier (0.60). This is a verifier-only false-negative, not a rubric defect — rubric text is mechanism-agnostic and admits 'any behaviorally equivalent transformation'.
_self_critique_log.fix: Patched tests/test.sh inside the SCALE_OK Python block: (1) added a helper_values map alongside helper_refs to retain the ast value node for each in-inner helper assign; (2) added a _load_targets_ptr(value, ptr_name) AST scan that returns True when the value contains a tl.load whose first argument transitively references the bare Name K_scale_ptr (or Q_scale_ptr) — covering tl.load(K_scale_ptr), tl.load(K_scale_ptr + offs), and tl.load(K_scale_ptr).to(tl.float32); (3) constructed k_scale_terminals / q_scale_terminals sets pre-populated with the canonical names and extended with any helper variable whose RHS satisfies _load_targets_ptr; (4) rewrote resolve() to treat any name in those terminal sets as a terminal, returning the canonical 'k_scale' / 'q_scale' token so downstream has_q / has_k checks remain unchanged. Net effect: rename-style fixes (k_scale -> k_scale_vec, k_scale_loaded, k_scale_block, etc.) now resolve correctly. No change to rubric text — rubric was already rename-tolerant via the 'e.g.' mechanism framing. No change to other gates (classify_file / no_buggy_pair use is_chained which already passes on renames because chained-pattern detection misses the renamed names, which is the intended behavior for those gates).
